justification: All pass criteria met: 8 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering happy path (AC-1, AC-2), already-shipped/delivered rejection (AC-3, AC-4), already-cancelled idempotency (AC-5), plus authorization and validation edge cases. 6+ explicit ambiguities surfaced in low_confidence_areas and 4 detailed escalations covering cancellable states, refund policy, courier compensation, and partial cancellation. Confidence level is 40, well below 65 threshold. Business rules are clearly labeled with assumptions in the confidence section rather than presented as confirmed facts. Edge cases section is thoroughly populated with 7 scenarios.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough acceptance criteria — 8 scenarios far exceed the minimum 3, covering happy path, edge cases, authorization, and validation
Confidence level of 40 accurately reflects the severely underspecified input
4 well-structured escalations with options and recommendations, each clearly typed as 'ambiguity'
Explicitly flags 'who can cancel' via BR-3 and AC-6, and notes cancellation window ambiguity in escalations
Assumptions are clearly labeled as assumptions in the confidence section, not presented as confirmed business rules
Edge cases section covers race conditions, double-cancellation, refund failures, partial cancellation, and system outages

BR-3 states 'Only the order owner may cancel' as an invariant business rule, but this is an assumption — the spec doesn't clarify whether operations staff can also cancel. While flagged elsewhere, the invariant label is slightly overconfident
BR-6 (mandatory cancellation reason) is presented as a rule rather than purely as an assumption, though it is marked invariant:false and listed in low_confidence_areas

agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation is a critical lifecycle event in logistics and courier operations. A user requesting cancellation triggers a cascade of business processes: halting fulfillment, initiating refund logic, releasing inventory or courier capacity, and notifying downstream systems. The single sentence 'Users should be able to cancel their order' leaves nearly every material business decision unspecified.","bounded_context":"Order Management","ubiquitous_langu
